title: Trauma and the Body's Wisdom: Learning to Trust Your Somatic Signals
excerpt: Discover how trauma impacts your body and learn to trust your somatic signals for deeper healing and nervous system regulation.
body:
Introduction: Why the Body Holds the Key to Trauma Recovery

For many who have experienced trauma, traditional talk therapy alone often leaves a gap — a lingering sense that something is still unresolved. This is because trauma isn't just stored in the mind; it’s encoded deeply in the body. Pioneers in trauma research like Bessel van der Kolk and Peter Levine have shown that the body holds a critical wisdom, often called somatic memory or somatic signals, that can guide us toward healing when we learn to listen.

This article explores trauma’s imprint on the body, why your somatic signals matter, and practical ways to start trusting and working with your body’s innate wisdom to move beyond stuckness.

What Are Somatic Signals?

Somatic signals are the physical sensations, impulses, and bodily responses that arise in reaction to internal states and external stimuli. They can be subtle — like a tightness in the chest, a flutter in the stomach, or a sense of heaviness — or intense, such as a racing heart or muscle tension.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory helps us understand these signals as expressions of our autonomic nervous system (ANS), which regulates our fight-flight-freeze responses and our social engagement system. Trauma disrupts this regulation, often leading to chronic states of dysregulation that manifest as somatic signals.

Somatic Signals Are the Body’s Language

When trauma occurs, the body doesn’t forget. Instead, it stores the experience as physical tension, impulses, or sensations that may be difficult to articulate verbally. According to Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, these sensations are the body’s attempt to complete an interrupted defensive response. Recognizing and following these signals allows the nervous system to discharge trapped energy and restore balance.

How Trauma Disrupts the Body’s Wisdom

Bessel van der Kolk, in his seminal book The Body Keeps the Score, explains how trauma hijacks the brain and body’s natural ability to regulate emotions and bodily states. Instead of a smooth flow between activation and rest, trauma survivors often get stuck in patterns of hyperarousal (fight or flight) or hypoarousal (freeze, shutdown).

This dysregulation clouds our ability to read somatic signals accurately. For example, a protective tightening of muscles might feel like chronic pain, or a freeze response might manifest as numbness or dissociation. The body’s signals become distorted or misinterpreted, leading to avoidance or disconnection instead of engagement.

Trauma and the Nervous System

  • Hyperarousal: Heightened alertness, anxiety, muscle tension.
  • Hypoarousal: Numbness, dissociation, low energy.
  • Disorganized states: Fluctuations between fight, flight, and freeze without resolution.

Deb Dana, a leading clinician and author on Polyvagal Theory, emphasizes that understanding this nervous system spectrum is crucial to recognizing your body’s signals as attempts to self-protect and heal.

Why Trusting Your Somatic Signals Matters

When trauma disrupts our internal cues, we lose trust in ourselves and our ability to respond to stress safely. Reconnecting with somatic signals rebuilds self-trust and empowers us to respond adaptively rather than reactively.

“The body tells the truth, even when the mind is confused.” – Bessel van der Kolk

Trusting your somatic signals means tuning in to what your body is communicating without judgment or suppression. This practice counteracts the common trauma pattern of disconnection and avoidance, replacing it with curiosity and presence.

Practical Steps to Reconnect and Trust Your Body’s Signals

1. Cultivate Body Awareness

Start with simple practices that bring attention to your bodily sensations. Mindfulness meditation focused on the body, gentle movement like yoga or Qi Gong, and breath awareness are effective starting points.

  • Notice areas of tension, heat, or numbness.
  • Observe how your body feels during different emotional states.
  • Practice regularly to build a vocabulary of sensations.

2. Learn to Differentiate Sensations

Not all somatic signals are equal — some indicate safety and presence, while others signal distress or activation. Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, teaches that distinguishing between these sensations helps in developing somatic regulation skills.

  • Ask yourself: Is this sensation activating or calming?
  • Is it localized or diffuse?
  • Does it change with breathing or movement?

3. Use Grounding and Resourcing Techniques

Deb Dana’s work on Polyvagal-informed therapy highlights the importance of resourcing — finding internal or external anchors that bring the nervous system back to safety. These could be sensations like feeling your feet on the ground, holding a comforting object, or recalling a safe memory.

4. Track and Journal Your Somatic Experiences

Writing down what you notice in your body and how it changes over time helps integrate somatic awareness into conscious understanding. Dan Siegel’s concept of "mindsight" — the ability to perceive your internal experience — is strengthened through this practice.

5. Seek Somatic-Based Therapeutic Support

While self-directed work is powerful, working with somatic therapists trained in methods like Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, EMDR (Francine Shapiro), or Internal Family Systems (Richard Schwartz) can deepen your ability to safely navigate somatic signals and trauma responses.

Common Challenges and How to Overcome Them

Building trust in your somatic signals is not always straightforward. Many trauma survivors experience:

  • Fear of sensations: Body sensations can feel overwhelming or frightening. Start gently and use grounding techniques.
  • Dissociation: Feeling disconnected from your body is common. Use small, incremental awareness practices.
  • Confusion about signals: Learning to interpret somatic cues takes time and patience.

Gabor Maté emphasizes compassionate self-acceptance as essential in this process. Remember: your body is trying to help, not harm.

The Science Behind Somatic Healing

Modern neuroscience validates somatic approaches to trauma healing. Research shows that body-based interventions can:

  • Reduce symptoms of PTSD and anxiety by regulating the autonomic nervous system (van der Kolk, 2014).
  • Change brain activity in areas related to emotional regulation and interoception (the sense of the internal body) (Craig, 2009).
  • Increase vagal tone, which is associated with improved stress resilience and social engagement (Porges, 2011).

These findings underscore why trusting and working with your somatic signals is not just a feel-good idea but a scientifically grounded path to recovery.

Conclusion: Your Body as a Guide, Not an Enemy

Trauma teaches us that the body can be both a site of wounding and a source of healing. Learning to trust your somatic signals restores an essential relationship with yourself that trauma tried to disrupt. It is through this embodied awareness that you reclaim your nervous system’s natural capacity for regulation, resilience, and well-being.

As you embark on this journey, remember the words of Peter Levine: “The wisdom of the body is the greatest teacher of healing.” Your body’s signals are the language of that wisdom — listen, learn, and heal.

faqs.q: What are somatic signals in the context of trauma?
faqs.a: Somatic signals are bodily sensations and impulses that arise from the nervous system, reflecting how trauma is stored and expressed physically.

faqs.q: How does trauma affect the autonomic nervous system?
faqs.a: Trauma disrupts the balance of the autonomic nervous system, causing states of hyperarousal, hypoarousal, or disorganization, which influence physical sensations and emotional responses.

faqs.q: Can I trust my body’s signals if I’ve experienced severe trauma?
faqs.a: Yes, though it takes patience and practice. Reconnecting with your body through gentle awareness and somatic therapies helps rebuild trust over time.

faqs.q: What are some effective ways to start tuning into my somatic signals?
faqs.a: Practices like mindfulness meditation, breath awareness, gentle movement, journaling, and working with a somatic therapist are effective ways to begin.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma affects the body and mind, offering insights into somatic healing.

metaTitle: Trauma and the Body's Wisdom: Trust Your Somatic Signals
